title: Teaching AI to Do Good

modules.title: Why AI Safety Is Everyone's Problem
modules.sub: Understanding why AI risks concern all of society, not just experts
modules.description: Learners examine real-world examples of AI failures and near-misses to grasp why safety is a shared societal concern. They assess common misconceptions and build a personal framework for why this topic demands broad public engagement.

modules.title: What Does AI Alignment Actually Mean?
modules.sub: Defining the gap between what AI does and what we want
modules.description: Learners unpack the technical and philosophical meaning of alignment, distinguishing it from related concepts like safety, ethics, and reliability. They apply key definitions to concrete scenarios to sharpen their understanding of what misalignment looks like in practice.

modules.title: When AI Optimizes for the Wrong Goal
modules.sub: Exploring how misaligned objectives produce harmful or absurd outcomes
modules.description: Learners investigate case studies where AI systems pursued their assigned objectives in ways that violated human intent. They analyze the underlying mechanisms — reward hacking, proxy gaming, and goal misgeneralization — and evaluate why these failures are structurally hard to prevent.

modules.title: Controlling Systems Smarter Than Us
modules.sub: Grappling with the challenge of overseeing increasingly capable AI
modules.description: Learners confront the core dilemma of controlling AI systems that may exceed human capabilities in reasoning and planning. They weigh proposed approaches — corrigibility, interruptibility, and oversight mechanisms — and debate their practical and theoretical limits.

modules.title: Specification Gaming and Unintended Behaviors
modules.sub: Recognizing how precise-seeming instructions still produce unexpected results
modules.description: Learners study documented instances of specification gaming, where AI systems satisfy the letter but not the spirit of their instructions. They practice identifying specification gaps in example scenarios and reflect on why writing robust objectives is fundamentally difficult.

modules.title: Global AI Safety Efforts and Organizations
modules.sub: Mapping the institutions and initiatives shaping AI safety worldwide
modules.description: Learners survey the landscape of organizations — academic labs, nonprofits, government bodies, and industry teams — working on AI safety research and governance. They compare different institutional approaches and evaluate where influence, funding, and expertise are concentrated.

modules.title: Catastrophic Risk: Hype Versus Reality
modules.sub: Separating credible long-term risks from speculation and science fiction
modules.description: Learners critically examine arguments for and against treating advanced AI as an existential or catastrophic risk. They weigh expert disagreements, assess the quality of evidence on each side, and develop a calibrated personal position on which risks warrant serious attention.

modules.title: What You Can Do About AI Safety
modules.sub: Translating awareness into meaningful personal and professional action
modules.description: Learners identify concrete ways to engage with AI safety — through career paths, advocacy, informed voting, organizational influence, or continued learning. They draft a personal action plan that reflects their role, resources, and level of commitment to contributing to safer AI development.

synopsis: As AI systems grow more capable, ensuring they reliably do what we actually want — and not something subtly or dangerously different — has become one of the most urgent challenges in technology. This course demystifies AI safety and alignment research so curious learners can understand the stakes and join an informed global conversation.
rationale: A corpus similarity of just 0.50 against 'Ethics Framework' — the lowest among the three gaps — combined with a solid demand signal of 6/10 shows that AI safety and alignment concepts remain almost entirely absent from public literacy curricula despite representing existential-level consequences for society.
